Transcrição Completa: How To Rapid Scale 11 30 24     [00:00] "All right. So, we had a weekly call request. This week was from Noah and Alim. A breakdown of your perfect or most ideal high ticket funnel for rapid scaling step by step that you're seeing right now. Opt-in, no opt-in, mini VSSL, DSL, maximizing self-booking rates."   [00:20] "Alim said to follow up with that, asking the right questions on applications. So, we'll lump that into what we're going to call the best scenarios and assets to have in place for rapid scaling."   [00:35] "There is a succinct system that you must follow, give or take what it is that you're selling, and it does make a tremendous difference to your ability to dump a fuckload of ad spend into these ads managers and actually profit from it successfully while you're doing so in kind of a blitz fashion."   [00:55] "So, first of all, rapid scaling. Let me just initially say this just to get us started. It is a choice to scale extremely quickly. And it can break a tremendous amount of things in the instance that you are not prepared for the game of scaling up quickly."   [01:15] "There's a slow and sustainable way to make things happen and there is an ultra fast way to make things happen. And those differences can be tremendous for the things that happen, what you need to anticipate, what you need to prepare for and what you need to do."   [01:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Foundation of Deleveraging Risk"   NÚMEROS:  3 business managers mencionados   VISUAL:  Estrutura de preparação para scaling, conceito de guerra chest, múltiplos sistemas de backup   [01:40] "So it all kind of starts with first of all a foundation of deleveraging risk. You guys remember that call we did a few weeks ago where I talked about what you need in place like three business managers, multiple payment methods, Facebook invoicing, the Fox tag in order to delay or disable disapprovals from AI on your ad account and on your ads."   [02:05] "This is like the foundation to rapid scaling. First of all, like before you actually initiate it, you need to make sure that you have everything in place. It's as if you were going to go to war and you didn't already have a war chest full of money."   [02:20] "You didn't already have a surplus of ammo, an excess of guns, an excess of military-aged men who are capable of going on your country's behalf. Imagine going into a war unprepared. Very little ammunition, very little weaponry, very little people to wage the war. How well would you do in the war?"   [02:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Credit Lines & Financial Preparation"   NÚMEROS:  $100,000 credit line, $4,000/day, 25 days duration, 3 months timeline   VISUAL:  Exemplo matemático de scaling progression, timeline de pagamentos, comparação de velocidade de crescimento   [02:50] "The equivalent of that when you're rapid scaling, the foundation starts off with a few key things. Again, deleveraging risk being the main one. This includes having access to substantial credit lines."   [03:05] "You have to factor in the speed in which you're going to scale mathematically and you have to be able to say - I'll give you a great example like we have a client that 3 months ago, they wanted to start rapid scaling and we did the math at the time they were at like 4K a day."   [03:25] "At 4K a day with let's use the example of having a $100,000 credit line, you could have a tremendous amount of time pass before you actually need to pay off that credit card bill. $4,000 a day would be about 25 full days if you had $100,000 credit line."   [03:45] "Now, let me be clear in saying this. If your sales cycle is longer than two weeks and you only have a little more than three weeks to be able to pay off that said credit card, if the people from the first week and a half don't close by that third week, then you end up in a position where you're having to front more cash."   [04:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Plum Card from AMEX - Payment Structure"   NÚMEROS:  10% within 30 days, 90% within 60 days, $200,000-$300,000 monthly spend, 1.5% discount   VISUAL:  Estrutura de pagamento em camadas, timeline de 30-60-90 dias, sem limite de gasto preset   [04:15] "A good example of this for a card that can come in handy is the Plum from AMEX. It gives you the ability to pay 10% of the credit balance within the first 30 days and it gives you the ability to pay the other 90% of the credit balance within the following 60 days."   [04:35] "AMEX cards generally come when they're business cards with no preset spending limits. There will be a time in your future with AMEX most likely where they do an audit of your business financials including bank statements, tax returns, etc. That's typically when they put hard credit limits on your account."   [04:55] "As an example, like my Plum card, I've had no spending limit on it for years now. I've spent literally upwards of like $200 to $300,000 in a month on the Plum. Downside is you don't get any points."   [05:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Facebook Invoicing & Net 30 Terms"   NÚMEROS:  Net 30 payment terms, 30-day spending window   VISUAL:  Comparação entre plataformas (Facebook, TikTok, LinkedIn, Google), estrutura de pagamento invoice-based   [05:20] "Most of the platforms nowadays, including Facebook, TikTok, even LinkedIn does this, but LinkedIn you have to spend some money. Google AdWords is much less probable to do what I'm about to describe, but it's Facebook invoicing."   [05:35] "Facebook invoicing meaning I can operate on a net 30 with Facebook. I also have the ability to pay via wire. So, I'm essentially allowed to spend for that entire 30-day duration and I don't have to pay it until the end of the following 30-day duration."   [06:00] "So, Facebook invoicing can also be another way to offset risk onto somebody else instead of you having to take on the risk yourself, fronting your own cash out of your account in real time to pay it off with whatever the credit card billing cycle is for you."   [06:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Deleveraging Risk Infrastructure"   NÚMEROS:  Multiple business managers, multiple ad accounts, single pixel per business manager   VISUAL:  Estrutura completa de backup systems, múltiplas camadas de proteção, arquitetura de redundância   [06:30] "Real quick one in the chat if that makes sense. I have an entire call that we did on deleveraging risk with multiple bank accounts, multiple payment processors, multiple business managers, multiple ad accounts within those business managers, a single pixel per business manager that you add onto every single page."   [07:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Facebook Special Ad Category Updates - December/January 2025"   NÚMEROS:  December 2025, January 2025 implementation   VISUAL:  Alertas sobre mudanças vindas, restrições de financial services/health/wellness, domain-level restrictions   [07:05] "We'll also do a call - I think it's really important to talk about this. There's some new updates coming to Facebook potentially as soon as December, but most probable to be in January of 2025 here soon where they're going to be updating financial services, health, and wellness as a special ad category."   [07:25] "It's specific and isolated to the domain level and domains will no longer send back the standard events that they consider what's called a restricted standard event."   [07:45] "Right now it's all speculation, but it's coming from reps within the company that are high level. So, accurate speculation nonetheless, but it's still in development and it's still not finalized, set in stone."   [08:00] "As an example, like you might not be able to send schedule as a standard event back to the pixel if you are operating in financial services, health or wellness. This is the first time that we've seen a restriction based on a special ad category denying data coming back from a pixel."   [08:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Domain Rotation Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  2-3 backup domains needed   VISUAL:  Estratégia de rotação de domínios, eventos auxiliares (donate, app install, search), backup systems   [08:30] "With a client that we have that already experienced the domain getting the notification they can't send back data. All they had to do is just rotate the domain. So, they had to have essentially a second and third domain just like we talked about on our deleveraging risk call, just on standby with all the funnels built out on it."   [08:50] "In the meantime, we also added some auxiliary standard events that are going to be considered quote non-restricted such as donate, app install, and search. So, we placed those three standard events onto the call confirmation page that qualified people go to."   [09:15] "That way we can optimize around those standard events where we'll have qualified people conditioning those standard events in between now and a potential domain block for those otherwise restricted standard events like schedule or complete registration."   [09:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Scaling Timeline Mathematics"   NÚMEROS:  $4,000/day to $11,000/day, 25 days to 9-10 days payment window, 3-month progression   VISUAL:  Progressão matemática de scaling, compressão de timelines, impacto em cash flow   [09:40] "This is another example of deleveraging risk. When you are going into rapid scale, that company was spending $4,000 a day. Well, within three months they were going to be spending $11,000 a day. So that would take their timeline of when they'd have to pay their credit card balances from 25 days all the way down to about 10 days at some point even 9 days."   [10:05] "So it's like you have to anticipate these things. You have to be able to get approved and just have these credit lines sitting there. And in addition to that, you have to be able to get things like Facebook invoicing or an AMEX Plum and many backup cards."   [10:25] "So when you are actually at the point of scale as a result of rapid scaling, you still haven't accumulated additional risk. You successfully from the beginning as a foundational best practice chose to delever risk."   [10:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Funnel Selection Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  Scenario 1: 1 funnel, Scenario 2: 2-3 funnels (chunk method)   VISUAL:  Comparação visual entre one-funnel method vs chunk method, barras de receita divididas   [11:00] "After you get to a point where you've deleveraged risk, and that would mean you have a few business managers set up, you have a few ad accounts, you have a few different credit cards, you've ideally gotten approved for Facebook invoicing, you have cards like the Plum from AMEX, you've gotten key staff members identified."   [11:25] "Now, you get to the point where you're ready to start spending money. Here's where we have to determine what the funnels are going to be. There's a few ways to do it. There's the singular approach where you typically have one funnel and then there's the approach where you might have upwards of like two to three different funnels where you're following the chunk method."   [11:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "One Funnel Method - Revenue Bar Example"   NÚMEROS:  $1M/month target, 100% from single funnel   VISUAL:  Barra de receita única, exemplo Dan Lok 2018, $2.5M/month achievement, 70% email/30% remarketing split   [11:55] "The chunk method - let's use the example that we're trying to hit million-dollar months and this is our revenue bar for how we make that million dollars a month. In scenario one, if you did it with one funnel, literally all of the revenue would come from one specific funnel."   [12:15] "This would be comparable to what I did with Dan Lok in 2018 where we had one funnel. It was a book funnel where about 70% of the overall revenue came from an email, a 12 sequence email and the other 30% of the revenue came from remarketing ads pushing everybody to a webinar."   [12:35] "The email sequence and the remarketing ads all pushed people to the same two-hour long webinar. It was the same funnel to be clear as the conversion mechanism that got him up to $2.5 million a month."   [12:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Chunk Method - Multiple Revenue Streams"   NÚMEROS:  $600K call funnel, $300K webinar, remainder DM ads/low-to-high ticket, total $1M/month   VISUAL:  Barra de receita dividida em 3 segmentos, distribuição visual de diferentes funnels   [13:00] "This would be the equivalent of like more than half of our clients have singular funnel scaling events where they have one call funnel. They're running one specific strategy like the Hydra as an example or DM ads."   [13:20] "Whereas in scenario two where you do the chunk method, you might have a few different variable funnels that are contributing revenue to the overall mix of million-dollar months. You might have a call funnel, you might have a webinar, and you might have like DM ads that are all contributing."   [13:40] "Maybe this does like 600K in a month for you, your webinar does like 300K and your DM ads or whatever else you might have here like low ticket to high ticket is contributing the remainder."   [14:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "One Funnel vs Chunk Method - Risk Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  1 funnel = 1x attention, 3 funnels = 3x attention/work   VISUAL:  Comparação de foco e atenção, isolamento vs diversificação, KPIs multiplicados   [14:05] "From a perspective of deleveraging risk, you can still have a one-funnel method where you scale the shit out of one particular funnel. It does isolate all the attention to that one funnel. And technically, just from the perspective of we're chasing one thing versus we're somebody that's chasing many things, it takes our attention and it isolates it onto one place."   [14:30] "Which typically gets a better result and goes much farther when we just have that singular thing that we're focused on."   [14:45] "If you do the one-funnel method when you're rapid scaling, you typically have more of your attention that perfects that one thing, you have a singular set of funnel statistics to focus on, keep your attention on, and ensure are within KPI."   [15:05] "Versus here it's like you still have the same funnel metrics and KPIs that you have to focus on, but you have to focus on it three times over for three completely different offers. You have three completely different set of funnels, call to action ads, hammer them strategy ads."   [15:25] "You have just three times as much that you have to actively focus on as the marketer, and the business as a whole has three times the amount of stuff to focus on."   [15:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Rapid Scaling Reality Check"   NÚMEROS:  100% dos casos = one funnel method para blitz scaling   VISUAL:  Ênfase em foco singular, velocidade maximizada, atenção concentrada   [15:45] "Technically, you'd assume the chunk method would be safer from a diversification perspective. And to be fair, I also agree that that's the case. But when you talk about rapid scaling and you isolate the attention to which of these two scenarios is more probable to help you scale faster, it's the one funnel method for sure."   [16:05] "No exaggeration 100% of the time when we're trying to absolutely blitz the amount of revenue we can make for our clients or for our businesses in a given month, we're scaling the living shit out of one funnel."   [16:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Deleveraging Risk Within One Funnel"   NÚMEROS:  Multiple ad channels, multiple foundation campaigns   VISUAL:  Estrutura de múltiplos canais alimentando um funil único, camadas de proteção dentro de estratégia singular   [16:30] "To be clear, we can still delever risk in the one-funnel approach by having multiple ad channels and having multiple foundation campaigns contributing to the overall revenue and success of that singular funnel."   [16:45] "So, although yes, risky in the sense that it's a singular funnel, it's not risky if we have multiple campaigns, multiple channels contributing to it as well."   [17:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Funnel Types for Rapid Scaling - Ranked"   NÚMEROS:  #1 Call Funnels, #2 Webinars, #3 DM Strategies   VISUAL:  Lista ranqueada por facilidade de scaling, todos otimizados para call bookings em high ticket   [17:15] "Now, when you look at what type of funnels you're actually using to rapid scale, there are funnels that are far easier to be able to scale up quickly. Number one being call funnels, webinars - I want to be fair in saying these webinars are typically still optimized for call bookings."   [17:35] "They're not necessarily, especially when you're selling something high ticket, pushing towards a direct buy through the webinar itself. DM strategies, again, these are just the most common that we see amongst our high ticket clients."   [17:55] "These are also still optimized for calls and pushing people just through an additional step being setters in most instances in the DMs itself over to the call booking for the closers."   [18:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Financial Model Comparison: 1-Call vs 2-Call Close"   NÚMEROS:  $7,500 price point, $15,000 marketing costs, 20% sales commission, $120,000 total ad spend, 1,250 calls   VISUAL:  Duas tabelas lado a lado comparando one-call close vs two-call close, múltiplas colunas de KPIs   [18:20] "This in order is definitely listed as such where it's most - it's in most instances within this specific flow. Call funnels being the greatest. To be fair, it really just comes down to math."   [18:40] "So, when you go out there and you look at just examples, this is the financial model for a two call close that you see up here at the top. And then at the bottom here, you see for a one call close."   [19:00] "This is using the example of a $7,500 price point that you're cash collecting. It uses the example you have $15,000 in marketing costs. It uses the example you have a 20% commission to your salespeople."   [19:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Two-Call Close Model Breakdown"   NÚMEROS:  Cost per call, Show rate, Booking rate to 2nd call, 2nd call show rate, Close rate   VISUAL:  Fluxo de conversão em múltiplas etapas, friction points visualizados   [19:25] "And it uses the example you're only taking three expenses off in this case: Ad spend, marketing fees, sales commissions for what you see here calculated in the gross profit column."   [19:40] "From there, between the two models, it's also pretty simple. We have cost per call, show rate, and close rate. And then up here, we have cost per call, show rate for the first call, booking rate to the second call, second call show rate, and close rate."   [20:00] "I also tried to skew the statistics in the second call close model here for being very favorable to make the outcome of gross profit very similar between the one call and the two call."   [20:20] "Although to be fair, in almost all instances, a two call close typically diminishes the overall gross and net that you produce as a result of adding additional friction."   [20:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Capacity Planning Question"   NÚMEROS:  1,250 calls/month, 41 calls/day (7 days), 62.5 calls/day (5 days), 7-8 closers needed   VISUAL:  Cálculo matemático de capacidade de equipe, breakdown de calls por dia/semana   [20:45] "Let me be clear in saying this, first of all, these are just lumpsum metrics. This does not represent what I'm spending per day by how much I intend to go up per day and whether I'm going to forcefully go up to that or if I'm going to do like an if this then that scenario for when I scale what."   [21:10] "If I start and I'm rapid scaling, let's use the example at a slightly uncomfortable level. When it comes down to like what to actually do to start, this is important to understand. We're typically starting at a higher amount of daily spend in order to max out current stats."   [21:35] "Let's use the example that back in this math over here - how many of your closing teams right now could handle 1,250 calls booked into them within a month? 1,250 calls over 30 days is a total of 41 calls a day."   [21:55] "So simply put, if you're able seven days a week to take 41 calls, let's use the example that your staff's only taking calls 5 days a week. That'd be 62 and a half calls per day. Who here can take between 45 and 62 calls per day right now?"   [22:20] "If I have 62 calls a day, from assuming my closers are only working five days a week, and assuming they're one-hour calls on average, and assuming your closers have eight hours worth of 1-hour call slots available, that's essentially seven closers. It's about seven to eight closers."   [22:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Simulating Scale - Stress Test Philosophy"   NÚMEROS:  Poll results showing most cannot handle 45-62 calls/day   VISUAL:  Conceito de simulation testing, identificação de breaking points antes de real scaling   [22:50] "With rapid scaling, the initial goal should be that we trust our people and we want to see if anything breaks. We're essentially trying to see - we're going to do a simulation. We want to simulate scale by maxing out our current capacities."   [23:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Alim's Capacity Example"   NÚMEROS:  25 call slots/day, 6 days/week, 600 calls/month, $120,000 ad spend, $200 cost per call   VISUAL:  Cálculo específico baseado em capacidade real, math breakdown detalhado   [23:20] "Somebody tell me how many call slots you can actually take per day here. Alim in the chat says 25 call slots per day. How many days per week are your closers working? Six."   [23:40] "25 times 6 times 4 that puts you at 600 calls for the month. So in this example, if we essentially just cut this amount of ad spend in half, we'd go from the 250 down to about 125. That'd be 625 in total."   [24:05] "Now, to be clear, this is assuming a $200 cost per call. This is assuming a 70% show rate in both models. In the one call close, it assumes a 15% close rate. In the two call close, 65% second call booking, 60% second show rate, 38% close rate."   [24:30] "When you're spending $120,000 over the course of 30 days, you're looking at about $4,000 a day. How much are you spending right now, Alim? $2,000. So, you're about halfway there."   [24:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Value of Instant Doubling - What Would You Learn?"   NÚMEROS:  $2,000/day to $4,000/day, 300 calls to 600 calls/month   VISUAL:  Conceito de stress testing, learning opportunities através de maximum capacity   [24:55] "Right now, you're only getting about 300 calls per month, assuming a $200 cost per call. What would the value be of instantly doubling your calls? What would you find out if you instantly doubled your calls for a few days and you actually maxed out your closers?"   [25:15] "Alim: I think I'd learn that our marketing needs work. Like our marketing doesn't get the right people because there's a lot of entrepreneurs that come through our funnel and we need the setters to set them. Otherwise, we'd be trying to sell to a lot of entrepreneurs that are unqualified."   [25:40] "What else? Somebody else unmute yourself. What would happen if Alim doubled his spend from 2,000 a day to 4,000 a day, therefore maxing out his closer capacity per day? What else could break?"   [25:55] "The fulfillment on the back end. That's another one."   [26:05] "I actually have some experience doing this where I doubled - I did the math. I'm like, 'Oh, I can just double a closer.' And then I doubled it and the closer was not prepared for it and then he had a little bit of a meltdown. So, I think some closers not mentally prepared for double the calls."   [26:30] "Chris: I would say call quality and follow-ups tend to drop. I mean, it's an opportunity though, right? It's going to force us to grow as a team. So, I love the idea of doing this in spikes and then reviewing and then pushing the limit."   [26:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Four Main Stress Test Categories"   NÚMEROS:  4 áreas principais: Operations, Marketing, Sales, Risk   VISUAL:  Quatro categorias de breaking points, ênfase em identificação sistemática   [27:00] "When you run essentially a simulated scale by maxing out current capacity, just as most of you acknowledge, there's many instances in which you're flexing stress points. You're going to see potential breakage in operations."   [27:20] "Which I just want to be fair in saying is technically a great outcome because that would mean the sales and marketing divisions of your business passed the stress test and therefore handed the problem off to the only time operations really sees an issue, which would be after a fuckload of people get sold."   [27:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Hurricane Analogy - Hidden Problems"   NÚMEROS:  Regular rain vs hurricane-level rain   VISUAL:  Metáfora de leak detection, stress testing reveals hidden weaknesses   [27:50] "I have this little townhouse down here in Coconut Grove. It's covered in trees. When it rains it feels like a tropical rainforest. I remember during hurricane season there were many instances where it would rain really really hard."   [28:10] "During regular rain, there's no real problems that you can notice. I started noticing during hurricane season when there was really aggressive rain, there was one spot specifically where I found a leak."   [28:30] "I didn't notice a leak at all during the regular rain, but when there was a hurricane amount of rain, all of a sudden we had a leak. Sometimes you don't even notice that things are broken until you stress test them and you put more water into that same area and then it's revealed you have a problem."   [29:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "DM Strategy Case Study - Setter Capacity Failure"   NÚMEROS:  350 DMs/day claimed capacity, $10K-$20K/week normal, $40K/week stress test, 10% booking rate floor, dropped to 5%, 5-6 week test period   VISUAL:  Gráfico de booking rate vs spend, identificação de real capacity limits   [29:05] "We had a consulting client who was adamant absolutely adamant about the fact that in their DM strategy they could handle 350 DMs per day. That was what the guy mathematically quantified as the setter manager."   [29:25] "When we started scaling the shit out of the DM strategy, we got up to about 40,000 a week in the DM strategy and the normal call booking rate when they were spending somewhere between 10,000 and 20,000 a week was about 10%."   [29:45] "On great weeks, it'd be 15 to 20%. But the floor, the acceptable floor for the KPI was 10%. When we stress tested scaling by going to 40k a week, the booking rate went all the way down to 5%."   [30:05] "The setter manager all of a sudden started saying, he didn't acknowledge responsibility that they couldn't handle 350 a day. He started blaming lead quality. He started saying, 'Oh, the leads diminished.' I was not sold on that."   [30:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Systematic Testing - Finding Real Capacity"   NÚMEROS:  Week 1: $20K (11%), Week 2: $19K (11%), Week 3: $18K (15%), Progressive $1K drops, Below $18K = consistent 10%+ booking rate   VISUAL:  Week-over-week progression chart, systematic reduction revealing true capacity threshold   [30:35] "Here's the thing that happens. When you also simulate scale by maxing out, you get feedback that is potentially inaccurate. The setter manager said, 'Well, lead quality dropped dramatically. That's why the booking rate dropped.' I said, 'Bullshit. They can't handle 350 a day.'"   [31:00] "He did not want to hire more people. So therefore, I could not get my stress test thesis proven for whether more people would solve the problem. So instead, I had to scale back for several weeks."   [31:20] "This was about a five-week test that I had to run where every week I pulled it all the way back to about 20,000 in spend. I saw their booking rate went right back up in that following week to 11%. I then dropped it by $1,000 a week."   [31:40] "So I went from 20,000 a week to 19,000 a week. Stayed at about 11% for the booking rate. I then went to 18,000. Booking rate went up to 15%. I then went down to 17, 16, 15."   [32:00] "Over like a good five, six week period of time, week over week, dropping it by $1,000. Every time I would drop it below $18,000, the booking rate percentage would go up dramatically by anywhere from 1 to 3%."   [32:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Thesis Proven - Hiring More Setters"   NÚMEROS:  At $18K/week: 150-175 DMs/day, $30K/week: sustainable with more setters, booking rate held above 10%   VISUAL:  Prova de tese, correlation between team capacity and performance sustainability   [32:30] "Does that still prove my thesis? My thesis being there's a level here that's clearly above $20,000 in a week of spend where you have to hire more setters. And if you fail to hire more setters, then we will not be able to scale the DM funnel."   [32:50] "Somewhere between anywhere below 18,000 is clearly their capacity. At 18,000, they were getting anywhere from like 150ish to about 175 total people in a day for DMs per day. And their booking rate sustained above the 10% no matter what."   [33:15] "After my thesis was proven, the guy goes out, he hires two more setters. Then, we flux up again. We simulate scale by maxing it out. We push up the spend to about $30,000. And guess what happens? Like fucking magic, the booking rate stays above 10%."   [33:35] "But if I scaled it above $30,000, it would diminish again. So we found out the real daily volume that the current capacity, the current quantity of setters could handle was less than half of what the setter manager was telling the business it could actually handle."   [34:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Point of Scaling - KPI Maintenance"   NÚMEROS:  Emphasis on holding KPIs while scaling   VISUAL:  Conceito central: scale sem diminishing returns, KPIs como foundation   [34:05] "There's no point in scaling if all the key KPIs diminish as you scale because you're just literally spending more money to typically make the same or less as what you were spending at a lower level."   [34:20] "The point of scaling is to make a fuckload more money. But in order to make a fuckload more money, your KPIs have to hold as you scale."   [34:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Acceptable KPI Flux - Financial Impact Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  Cost per call: $200 to $225 (+$25), Show rate: 70% to 60% (-10%), Cash collected: $7,500 to $6,500 (-$1,000), Combined impact: ~$150K difference   VISUAL:  Tabela comparativa mostrando impacto cascata de múltiplas KPI changes   [34:45] "The intention of this is to ensure that our KPIs hold. If our KPIs do not hold, it's okay to be fair if they flux a little bit because we can quantify how much they can flux by for us to still make a desirable amount of money."   [35:05] "If Alim goes from the 300 total calls that he's at right now up to the 600, he grosses 466, he grosses 472 in the one call. He gross profits 238 and 243. Let's just change one number."   [35:25] "Let's say that when he scaled from the 300 calls and let's assume his cost per call was 200, let's just say it goes to 225. He can handle 225. A little flux like that is nothing too significant for him."   [35:45] "It diminishes the overall ROI by a little bit. It's not by much. It's a little less than like $30ish,000 there."   [36:00] "Let's say that at the same time, his salespeople for the first time being maxed out really start to experience an inability to do basic things that they were otherwise capable of doing prior where they had to send selfie videos."   [36:20] "His cost per call went from 200 to 225 and his initial call show rate went from 70 down to 60 because they couldn't handle some of the best practices they needed to follow. Now we're seeing almost a $100,000 difference in the overall amount of revenue that the business is producing."   [36:45] "Almost an $80,000 difference in the amount that we are gross profiting here. Let's use the example that at the same time, the salespeople no longer had the capacity to train as often as they were."   [37:05] "The sales manager, he's already having to go out there and hire new people in order to try to keep up with the scale that they're operating at. So therefore, as a result of the less volume of training, less attention from the sales manager, the cash collected per sale went from 7500, it suffered $1,000 loss down to 6,500."   [37:30] "Now we're talking almost $150,000 difference between a very subtle flux of three key KPIs."   [37:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Tipping Point - When Lower Spend Wins"   NÚMEROS:  $60K spend scenario vs $120K spend scenario, maintaining KPIs can equal higher spend with worse KPIs   VISUAL:  Comparação de cenários, conceito de optimal spend level vs maximum spend level   [37:55] "It's like technically if he had less overall calls and therefore spent half as much. If he spent $60,000, but he maintained better KPIs, like, yeah, I mean, arguably, he could actually still make as much."   [38:15] "So, we have to monitor our KPIs as we scale. We also have to know the tipping point for like when it becomes worth it in spend, even if our KPIs flux a little bit."   [38:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Andrew's 16x ROAS Example"   NÚMEROS:  $3K/month spend, 16x ROAS, $48K revenue, vs $500K/month spend, 2x ROAS, $1M revenue, $200K net profit   VISUAL:  Comparação dramática entre high ROAS/low volume vs lower ROAS/high volume profitability   [38:40] "A good example of this, Andrew, unmute yourself and tell me what your ROAS was on that recent funnel you ran that was like what was it like 16 to 1 or something like that. Yeah, 16x. How much did you spend to get to 16x? I'm barely spending. That was like three grand a month."   [39:05] "As an example, right? Like he turned three grand into 48 grand. That's a 16x. Now, imagine he got attached to the idea that only $3,000 in spend is gonna hold at that level of ROAS."   [39:25] "If he spent half a million dollars a month, did a 2 to 1 ROAS, net a total of like 200 grand out of that total million dollars that he flipped it into. So he spent 500, grossed a million. Let's say he had $300,000 in additional expenses and still put 200k in his pocket."   [39:50] "Him getting a 2 to 1 ROAS and putting 200k in his pocket at that level is still a better idea than spending three grand and getting a 16x ROAS and putting the difference of maybe 30ish,000 in his pocket after his expenses."   [40:15] "There's a clear tipping point where, okay, somewhere between $3,000 and $500,000 in spend where your return goes from a 16x all the way down to a two, it's still wildly worth it comparatively to being attached to the $3,000 16xing into 48,000."   [40:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Quantifying The Tipping Point"   NÚMEROS:  Multiple spend scenarios: $100K increments, diminishing KPIs at each level, net profit calculations   VISUAL:  Financial modeling framework, systematic approach to finding optimal spend threshold   [40:45] "While we're obviously trying to get our KPIs to hold, I want you to also be able to quantify the tipping point. This is critical. After I stress test and I find out, is anything fucked here? Do I have any problems that occur in any of these areas?"   [41:10] "I can counteract some of the things I learned during the stress test. I can as an example conclude that if my show rate drops, I need to be more aggressive with the hammer them strategy."   [41:30] "If my feedback I get is, 'Hey, lead quality dropped a little bit when you spent more money,' then I might have - remember we talked about foundational campaigns."   [41:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Foundational Campaigns Explained"   NÚMEROS:  Multiple campaigns running simultaneously, each finding its ceiling   VISUAL:  Conceito de múltiplas campaigns atingindo tetos individuais, strategy de layer building   [41:55] "A foundational campaign means that as time passes and as scale occurs, there reaches a point where there's kind of a ceiling. And it's a hypothetical ceiling that you don't know exists until all of a sudden you hit it."   [42:15] "It essentially means if you scale beyond that amount within that campaign, you just spin the wheels. Like there's truly no additional upside to spending past a certain point in a given campaign."   [42:35] "It could be that that specific hook has a certain amount of bandwidth for that audience you're targeting. It could just be that maybe you've maxed out that specific audience and you need to add new audiences with that same hook you've already proven."   [42:55] "The goal of foundational campaigns is to add as many foundational campaigns that we can to what we're doing as we scale. So we have first of all deleveraged risk. We max out every campaign's performance to its hypothetical ceiling we eventually run into."   [43:15] "And then after we've reached that point, we just take our attention and try to put it into a new foundational campaign and we let those current ones sit there and run at the level that we found is their ceiling."   [43:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Simulating Scale - The Cycle"   NÚMEROS:  Pattern: Simulate → Break → Fix → Simulate again   VISUAL:  Gráfico de dente de serra mostrando ciclos de scale up/pull back/fix/scale up   [43:45] "When we're scaling and our lead quality all of a sudden diminishes when we attempt to scale, that also might mean that if we want to scale to a certain point, we have to start doing mass marketing or we have to find new in-market audiences to actively sell to."   [44:10] "When you simulate scale, here's how it might look. If that axis is money and the bottom one's time, you might have - Alim's at 300 calls. He simulates 600 calls, some shit breaks, he pulls it back down to 300, he tries to fix right here in particular."   [44:35] "This is like the prepare stage. This is like the fix stage. And then again, he simulates scale again. Something breaks. He comes back. He fixes it. You might repeat that same cycle however many times it takes until eventually you simulate this scale and no water leaks into your house from throwing a fuckload of water at it."   [45:00] "Nothing breaks during one of these key stress tests. What happens after that? We're officially rapid scaling once we've overcome the stress tests successfully."   [45:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Financial Modeling During Stress Tests"   NÚMEROS:  Various spend levels: $60K, $120K, $400K with corresponding ROI and net profit scenarios   VISUAL:  Multiple financial scenarios side by side, tipping point identification framework   [45:30] "While we are going through the stress tests and fixing what broke or was exposed to be a problem, we are attempting to quantify the tipping point where it becomes worth it to spend while we have diminishing KPIs."   [45:50] "How do we quantify the tipping point math? Financial modeling. In this scenario, we started with a $200 cost per call, a 70% show rate, and $7,500 cash collected."   [46:10] "If he goes from 60,000 in spend to 120,000 in spend, let's look at the differences here. If he goes from 120,000 in spend when he's actually ready to scale all the way up to $400,000 in spend."   [46:30] "The ROAS of him spending $120,000, it put about a hundred-ish,000 in his pocket. If he spends $400,000, it puts $400,000 in his pocket."   [46:50] "I want to quantify at each individual statistical level some room for every KPI to diminish, but if I throw enough spend at it, I still have a quantifiable point where I need to reach that level of spend where the diminishing KPIs don't actually matter anymore."   [47:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Financial Modeling Process"   NÚMEROS:  Step-by-step: Start with true KPIs → Add $100K → Diminish KPIs → Calculate gross/net → Repeat   VISUAL:  Processo sistemático de modeling, incremental testing approach   [47:20] "You sit here and you play around with the model where you start off with what I'm actually spending. Now, let me start off with my true KPIs for every statistic. Let me just throw an additional 100,000 at it, but diminish my KPIs a bit at each level."   [47:40] "And then let me just take note. Here's what that grosses. Here's what that nets. Now let me increase the spend by another 50 or 100,000. Here's what that grosses. Here's what that nets."   [48:00] "And there becomes a very obvious point in those notes where if I spent this, this looks like where it'd be worth. If I spent less, anything below that, I have to hold these specific KPIs to make it worth it up until the tipping point."   [48:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Noah's Ark Analogy - KPI Integrity"   NÚMEROS:  Wood boat surviving flood conditions   VISUAL:  Metáfora de estrutura holding under extreme pressure, KPIs como foundation structure   [48:30] "When I am rapid scaling and I officially start, I am under all circumstances trying to hold that fucking KPI where it's at until I reach the point where I have quantified the amount I need to spend, the call volume I need per day."   [48:50] "Obviously, it's ideal to be very clear with you that yes, the KPI still hold even after the tipping point. And that happens all the time, by the way. But I'm more so trying to make you aware that in all scenarios in scale, there is a tipping point that you do at least want to be aware of at the minimum."   [49:15] "I compare it to - everybody in your head real quick. Picture Noah's Ark. That thing was built out of fucking wood. The fabled story of a massive rainstorm. Two animals, male, female, added onto this boat."   [49:35] "Just imagine the literal flood. The ship was built out of wood. It had to hit - the fucking wood boat was ramming into buildings. It was ramming into mountain sides and it was holding together. If that thing would have broke, everybody would have died."   [50:00] "That's what I'm saying is the equivalent of these KPIs holding or not. If they failed to hold, you might as well just scale back down and have chocked it up as a stress test."   [50:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Stress Tests Can Look Like Scaling"   NÚMEROS:  60%+ of attempted rapid scaling = actually stress tests, <40% = actual successful rapid scaling   VISUAL:  Reality check sobre expectativas, maioria das tentativas são learning experiences   [50:30] "I want to be clear on what I just said. If in the instance you run a stress test and you thought okay I solved the problems here at this level everything solved and you now consider that this next level here was all the scale and it ended up just being a bigger stress test."   [50:50] "There's still nothing wrong with that. You just had - you just unfortunately again like yeah there might have been some heavy rain where here you thought you got all the leaks solved but in reality you had to have hurricane level rain and wind to expose the actual leak."   [51:15] "A lot of the times when you're rapid scaling believe it or not you're actually just unknowingly running heightened levels of stress tests."   [51:30] "Definitely more than half the time without exaggeration probably more than 60% of the time rapid scaling results in okay you're going to conclude it was a stress test and then you're going to try again the next time around."   [51:50] "Less than half of the time when you just attempt to rapid scale something, does it actually hold at the level that it was successfully performing at at lower levels."   [52:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Company Narrative - The Killer Mindset"   NÚMEROS:  Years between scaling attempts for many companies   VISUAL:  Conceito de false conclusions, self-limiting beliefs formados por stress test failures   [52:20] "There's a really really strong point I want to emphasize here before I continue, which is what we call company narrative. Company narrative means that when a company in this case does a stress test, but they articulated it to themselves internally as we're scaling."   [52:40] "If they're not in the frame of this could conclude as a stress test and we need to fix the things that broke as we scaled, what instead they conclude to is things don't work at that level and here's why. And they never attempt that level again."   [53:00] "It's a silly silly conclusion that prevents companies scaling all the time. It's very frequent where I come into an organization and I say okay when was the last time you tried to really press the gas and they tell me it was like years ago."   [53:20] "They were like this is the last time we really tried to punch it and here's what happened and it's like all I hear is that something little broke and they never really attempted to fix it and then stress test it again. They just concluded that that's how things are at that level."   [53:45] "This mindset tip of being in the mental frame of while I'm scaling, I'm looking for those breaking points and I'm never going to conclude that they can't be fixed and sustain at a heightened level. I have to scale it back down, fix it, and then I can scale back up."   [54:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "When You Must Fix at Scale - City Infrastructure Example"   NÚMEROS:  Week-long outages (hypothetical), real-time fixes required   VISUAL:  Analogia de infraestrutura crítica, water/power que não pode ser desligada   [54:20] "There are certain instances where things have to work at scale and you have to fix them in real time and it doesn't make sense to pull it back down. Give you a great example, city infrastructure."   [54:40] "Let's use the example that the main water line to your specific neighborhood breaks. How pissed would you be if they shut it off for a whole week, scaled it back down in order to fix it?"   [55:00] "Have you ever had an instance where you can think of your power going out for a full week, your water going out for a full week while they fix critical city infrastructure? And it probably never will because that type of problem, it has to be dealt with while it's still operating at scale."   [55:25] "When it comes down to money, people not having water, people not having power, they might go out and rob people or kill people. You are just going to run out of money if you can't fix your problem at critical scale."   [55:45] "So therefore, you have to reduce it in order to retain your bank balance. You might not be able to sustain the scale is what I'm attempting to emphasize here."   [56:05] "There's certain things, simply put, that you have to label as this is my version of the water has to stay on and I have to solve this under any circumstances no matter the scale I'm in. And there's other problems where you can quantify that they are going to fucking milk you dry if you sustain that problem for a long duration."   [56:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Q&A - Financial Modeling for Setters"   NÚMEROS:  Blended models: direct booking + setter booking + combined   VISUAL:  Abordagem de múltiplos modelos financeiros para diferentes fluxos   [56:40] "Ron: When you're in that financial model you're doing, you had it as cost per call. Is that a blended average of setter booked and self-booked calls or is it like split out?"   [57:00] "If you have setters that you can factor in as well, you would technically do a secondary set of financial modeling. So, you would have your direct booking model and then you'd have your separated model. You could also create a third model that's blended between the two."   [57:25] "I try to just provide you more with like a general model for reference. You'd want to make sure you actually model it out for whatever your business's statistics are. And if that means you have two different scenarios like that, you'd ideally model two and then blend a third."   [57:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Standard Scaling Levels - Alim's Example"   NÚMEROS:  2K to 4K daily spend, relative to individual business capacity   VISUAL:  Conceito de scaling levels sendo relativos à capacidade individual, não números absolutos   [58:00] "Alim: What are those spend levels that you see companies kind of flex to? Like 2K, 4K, 8K, like are there levels that you've seen that are pretty standard and consistent across companies?"   [58:20] "Here's the thing - for you, you're at half your closer capacity. If you risked for a good week and you could comfortably do it, maxing out your system, then that's the level that it is for you. It'd be 4K a day for your system."   [58:40] "If somebody else has two closers and they're at half of their current capacity, but their total scaled capacity would put them at like $1,000 a day, it's not like there's a static answer. It's whatever the number is is specific to your business."   [59:05] "You were being transparent in the group chat Alim about the stripe capital loan and your current bank balances for the company and where it would put you at. If you sustained $4,000 for seven days, that's a decent bit of money of your total bank balance."   [59:30] "Hopefully that wouldn't break everything and that you'd experience what is ideal in that scenario, which would be you make the money back. You have more than a 50% probability that something breaks. There's a higher probability you at least make the money back."   [59:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Stress Test Risk Reality"   NÚMEROS:  Low probability of total loss, high probability of at least breaking even   VISUAL:  Risk assessment framework, probabilidades realistas de outcomes   [59:55] "There's typically a very very low probability to be very clear with you guys that when you do a stress test, you don't at least make the money back that you put out during the stress test."   [60:15] "It's just can you at least be like whatever amount of profitability you are at the stress test is what actually matters. You're not risking the money and just vaporizing it. I don't want to make it sound like you're losing your bank buffer when you run a stress test."   [60:35] "But at the end of the day, that is still possible. Not necessarily as probable though."   [61:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Chris's Insight - Team Framing"   NÚMEROS:  20 years business experience mentioned   VISUAL:  Importância de framing para equipe, stress test como oportunidade vs ameaça   [61:05] "Chris: I've been operating my business for almost 20 years now. And I've gone through this cycle of getting excited and just not framing it properly for my team. I can't overemphasize it."   [61:25] "No matter what the team size is, really framing it like a stress test is such a big opportunity because people want to know that we're as leaders, we're pressing ahead and we're fearless leaders that are driving the company forward."   [61:45] "But they also want it to be in a way that they can see the light at the end of the tunnel and they're not as convicted about the vision almost ever as we are. So, I think that's huge to frame it in that light of how does this tie back to them personally."   [62:10] "Yeah. And on top of that, it generally prepares everybody, too, rather than just fire hosing a ton of new daily activity onto their plate."   [62:30] "You tell people, 'Hey, we're going to challenge the company here for this next x amount of days, and we're going to see if we can sustain it. We're going to see if we can handle it or not. And if we can, great we all have a greater capacity that we're going to maximize."   [62:50] "In the instance that something breaks, please what we're attempting to do during this is actively find out what does break and we want everybody to be very mindful and nobody's going to be criticized in a negative way."   [63:10] "We're actively trying to find out is something here fucked up. Is something broken? Does something need our attention?"   [63:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Vehicle Analogy - Finding Breaking Points"   NÚMEROS:  60-70 mph smooth, 120 mph shaking reveals problems   VISUAL:  Toyota Camry 1990 example, speed thresholds revealing hidden issues   [63:35] "I always give the analogy of the water test. Most people live in a place or have some type of analogy you can use for their home environment or even their vehicles."   [63:55] "You can drive a fucking Toyota Camry down the road from 1990 on the highway at 60 70 mph. If you push that thing up to 120 mph it might start shaking viscerally and you might find out it can't go that fast."   [64:15] "But sometimes you still have to push it up to 120 mph to find out that it shakes. Otherwise, it runs smooth every other mile per hour below a certain threshold. I'd rather know than not know."   [64:40] "You have everybody contribute to this internal log essentially of what's potentially breaking. The ideal scenario in reality is that you do the stress test and you just perpetuate it because nothing actually broke."   [65:00] "But a minority of the time, do you just get to rapid scale into something without something breaking."   [65:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Consistent Scaling vs Rapid Scaling"   NÚMEROS:  Up and down pattern vs steady climb   VISUAL:  Gráfico comparativo de duas abordagens de scaling, pros/cons de cada   [65:25] "Question: If this is the case where it goes like this and it's kind of like up and down, is it better to just scale more consistently or do you feel like it's worth it if you have that breakthrough and get to a new level of revenue?"   [65:45] "It's a lot easier to be able to solve the problems at scale without having to pull it back if you're consistently in a moderate way scaling up."   [66:05] "The downside is sometimes it still makes more sense to attempt to rapid scale when you are trying to push an offer into the market very quickly and create a competitive advantage and moat and capture that revenue while the opportunity exists."   [66:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Black Friday Example - Timing Opportunities"   NÚMEROS:  Guy in elevator: $30K credit line, $5K Chase Sapphire, needed $50K/day spend, normally $10-20K/month revenue   VISUAL:  Oportunidade de timing específico, necessidade de rapid scaling para janelas limitadas   [66:35] "I'll give you a great example of that. So, it's Black Friday weekend. There's probably people on this call who did this yesterday where they spent more money in a day than they've spent over months in the entire fiscal year on ad spend."   [66:55] "I was a guy in my elevator all week super excited telling me he's taking advantage of Black Friday for the first time. His credit line was like $30,000 in total and he had like $5,000 on a Chase Sapphire. He was like, 'Dude, I need to spend like $50,000 a day. How am I going to do it?'"   [67:15] "I was like, 'You're going to be paying your credit card balances in real time is how you're going to do it.' In a month he regularly makes like tens of thousands of dollars spending maybe at most like 10 to 20k."   [67:35] "He's never had that stress test break his systems. So my point being he's not prepared to put out that level of spend, but to take advantage of that specific day and weekend, he had to do it anyway, otherwise that opportunity doesn't exist for him during the entire rest of the year."   [68:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Andrew's Sober Living Homes - Competitive Moat Opportunity"   NÚMEROS:  Currently $3K/month spend, 16x ROAS, untapped market   VISUAL:  Oportunidade de first-mover advantage, comparação com Tom Cruz Section 8 example   [68:05] "Let's take Andrew's offer. I love Andrew's offer as a great example. He teaches a form of real estate I never heard of before - sober living homes."   [68:25] "You look out in the marketplace, how many other people are teaching about sober living homes? There's like next to none. But it's like Andrew is still over here fucking around spending $3,000, flipping it at 16x to 48 grand."   [68:45] "I bet there's at least two to three other people out there that are actively doing what Andrew is doing that are starting to see Andrew on YouTube that are thinking to themselves, 'I bet this guy's making some money with this info product. I've been doing this. I can do this, too, and I have more properties than Andrew does.'"   [69:10] "They're starting to build their personal brands. They might not do it in the organic way that Andrew's gotten comfortable with. They might do it in the paid advertising way. They might have a lot of business acumen, too."   [69:30] "They might start off with five closers. They might start off with $50,000 in ad spend and unbeknownst to Andrew, there could be somebody out there literally right now already doing their version of a stress test and seeing if they could rapid scale into it."   [69:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Tom Cruz Section 8 Case Study"   NÚMEROS:  First month: $700,000 revenue, demand waiting to be captured   VISUAL:  Exemplo de rapid scaling bem-sucedido, competitive moat creation   [70:00] "Just like Tom Cruz with section 8 with that offer that he had, I'll never forget that first month I launched Tom Cruz, $700,000. It was like the demand was just sitting there waiting for somebody to scale into it."   [70:20] "That makes a lot of sense that Andrew would start to rapid scale a bit because there's a competitive moat that he creates."   [70:40] "There's these two guys that live literally right beneath me. They do TikTok live hours every fucking day and they have only like 3,000, one of them has 16,000 followers on TikTok and all they talk about is the exact word for word of just replicating what Tom Cruz does for section 8."   [71:05] "I go into their live streams here and there and I'll observe the fact that in their comments in real time for the hundred-ish people they have watching them, Tom Cruz gets brought up every like five fucking comments."   [71:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Competitive Moat Power - Tom Cruz Dominance"   NÚMEROS:  $115M company exit competitor, 2,000 units purchased, still can't compete   VISUAL:  Poder de first-mover advantage, brand dominance impedindo competição   [71:30] "There was a guy here who has $115 million from a company exit. He got into section 8, bought 2,000 units at once, tried to teach section 8. All he gets is people saying, 'You're just copying Tom Cruz.'"   [71:50] "There's not a single person that could attempt. There were these two guys, the Kevin that teach Amazon. They started buying section 8. They tried to start teaching section 8. Again, littered every ad comment, every fucking TikTok that they ever talk about with section 8. Tom Cruz this, Tom Cruz that, Tom Cruz."   [72:15] "Andrew could be the Tom Cruz of Sober Living Homes if he stopped fucking around and did some rapid scaling. That's a sensible reason to rapid scale because somebody will be the Tom Cruz of that offer. And it's somebody who's going to fucking rapid scale."   [72:40] "That's what Tom did. And that paid off tremendously well for Tom. And for Tom it didn't break. Tom was one of the 40 percentile people I talked about where there was no stress test failure. It just went right to we're actually rapid scaling. We didn't have to pull back."   [73:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Three Main Reasons for Rapid Scaling"   NÚMEROS:  1. Timing windows (Black Friday), 2. Competitive moat creation, 3. Accelerated problem discovery   VISUAL:  Três categorias principais de justificativa para rapid scaling   [73:15] "When does it make sense to rapid scale? There's plenty of sensible use cases and reasons such as the Black Friday ecom example, such as I need to - there's nobody else doing this shit yet. I need to blitz this market and be the Tom Cruz in my market before somebody else does."   [73:40] "And at the same time, there's also scenarios where it's just a good reason being I want to stress test my systems and see if something breaks. Maybe I could be operating at a bigger level. I got some extra cash right now."   [74:00] "Let's take a risk and see if we can get this thing cranking and make some more money and just fix what's going to break faster rather than finding out because you're still going to find the same break points over a slower sustained duration of time."   [74:25] "What if I told you that you could take the same five things that would have broken over a year and compress it down to like a month worth of learning. You solve those problems within that next month."   [74:45] "Now, over the remaining 10 months out of that fiscal year, you can still technically slow and sustain your way to scale and know that you're not going to endure those problems because you already solved them. Therefore, giving you the ability to do like 30% scale a day instead of 10%."   [75:10] "Ron: I think the third one was actually the most impactful. The quicker you spend, the quicker you identify the problems, the quicker you can solve them. So, I think overall it just helps you."   [75:30] "It's exactly right. Yeah. And there's different reasons for everybody to do things, but to your point, I agree. The third reason is more universal."   [75:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Timing Question - December Scaling"   NÚMEROS:  December timing, 3-4 week sales cycles, Christmas week 3, New Year's week 4   VISUAL:  Considerações de timing sazonal, impacto de sales cycle em decisões de scaling   [76:00] "Question: Say we wanted to go into a - because I feel like we're doing pretty good at our current spend. I definitely want to test one more leg up. Would you recommend that December is not a good time to do that? Wait till January. What are some recommendations there?"   [76:25] "Great question in this sense that time of year can impact your success with your rapid scaling test. Let's use the example you have a three to four week sales cycle."   [76:45] "You can rapid scale throughout the first half of December and your results would be negligible in the second half of December when your sales cycle would usually finalize because the third week of December would be Christmas. The fourth week of December would be New Year's. People are fucked off during that time of year."   [77:10] "Based on your sales cycle timeline, that can definitely impact whether it's a smart play or not to scale during that time and attempt to figure it out. Factoring in your sales cycle timeline, let that be your answer. Different for everybody."   [77:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Q4 2024 Ad Market Observations"   NÚMEROS:  Some CPMs same as usual, some 2-3x higher, varied by demographic   VISUAL:  Market conditions analysis, variação por targeting demographic   [77:40] "What have been your observations this holiday season with ad prices? Some ad accounts are experiencing the same CPMs they do every other time of the year, which is quite interesting."   [78:00] "Some CPMs have literally doubled or tripled, give or take what demographic they're targeting. It's been about the same as every other year. I don't think there's been really any fluctuation."   [78:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Facebook Domain Blocking Announcement Impact"   NÚMEROS:  Financial services & health/wellness category restrictions, December/January 2025 rollout   VISUAL:  Maior impacto de Q4: anúncio de restrições vindas, não condições normais de mercado   [78:25] "The only disadvantage to this year has actually come as a result of that announcement of the financial services and the health and wellness category having domains blocked from sending back standard event data. But that has nothing to do with Q4. That's just an announcement they coincidentally made in Q4."   [78:50] "Everything else is pretty normal. I mean for us - every four years the black swan event of the election cycle occurs and this was that black swan event year."   [79:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Election Impact - Unknown Unknown"   NÚMEROS:  Day after Trump wins: 3 commercial roofing deals ($4M-$30M range) immediately approved   VISUAL:  Impacto eleitoral em decisões de compra high-ticket, spending confidence shifts   [79:20] "I was more relieved after the election had finalized. Not to get political here but to be very direct with you guys there was also a tremendous amount of money that was held up on the other side of one candidate winning and one candidate losing. That was very noticeable."   [79:45] "That was the biggest unknown unknown. I hadn't projected that if one candidate won and one candidate lost that somebody would or would not spend the money as a result."   [80:05] "A great example of that - day after Trump wins my first client ever who's still with us today, Lee Hait, he has his roofing company and he says the following morning after Trump had been announced later that night that he had won."   [80:25] "He goes, 'Had three commercial roofing properties as low as four million and as high as $30 million that have officially given us the check to proceed with their roof to get it done.' And they were not in any way, shape, or form intending on spending that money if Kamala had won in comparison."   [80:50] "Throughout that entire remainder of the week, every single client selling to rich people had extremely similar feedback from their sales reps coming through the chain of command that I was hearing about."   [81:10] "That was a big unknown unknown. I hadn't projected that that would be the case. I hadn't considered that that would be a reason for somebody to or to not do something like fix their roof of a commercial property or to buy an info product."   [81:35] "Long story short, thankfully we don't have to deal with that every year, but four years from now when that happens again, I will be more mindful of it."   [81:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Spending Into Events Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  Challenge ran end of October into election, higher CPMs but still profitable   VISUAL:  Philosophy de spending through market events vs pulling back   [82:00] "In one of your YouTube videos, you talked about spending into these kinds of things because I had a client where we ran a challenge literally like end of October. So it's like right coming up into the election and yeah, our CPMs were way higher, but we still spend into it and we still did well."   [82:25] "We're all about spending into things like this. We don't consider these events as something to sit back and just lose money during. It's always a game of math. If the ROI is there, you still spend."   [82:45] "You'd be surprised. There's a lot of businesses I talked to that clammed up that weren't going to do anything after that point or they were going to substantially contract as a result of just an election result."   [83:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Stress Testing vs Consistent Scaling - Percentage Rules"   NÚMEROS:  10-30% daily lift rule, Cost per X strategy alternative, 30% preferred for KPI maintenance   VISUAL:  Duas abordagens matemáticas para scaling, pros/cons de cada método   [83:15] "Joey: When it comes to stress testing versus consistently scaling what's the difference in terms of percentage if I assume that if I'm consistently scaling maybe I'm increasing the budget by 20% in case I'm stress testing am I going from 30 to 60 like doubling the budget?"   [83:40] "Great question. Your technical percentage of daily lift when you're rapid scaling can be as high as like doing the 30% in the 10 to 30% rule of scaling."   [84:00] "You could also just do the cost per X strategy to literally rapid scale. Cost per X strategy meaning, let's use the example, I have a $100 cost per call. Let's use the example I have 20 total call slots per day that I can fill."   [84:20] "I'm going to take those 20 total call slots times my $100 cost per call. My new daily budget's $2,000. I'm going to duplicate out that existing campaign. Set the daily budget at the $2,000, press publish, see what happens."   [84:45] "You could do either or. Preferably, you spend like a few days doing the 30% scaling rule so it's more realistic to what's going to happen when you scale."   [85:05] "Keep in mind higher probability that the KPIs hold, which is a big goal of rapid scaling when you do the 10 to 30% rule rather than when you do the cost per X rule."   [85:25] "I'd skew towards the 30% per day for sure. I would do that in the existing campaigns. If we're doing the 10 to 30% rule, we can keep it in the same campaigns that are currently running that we're trying to scale up."   [85:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Creative & Audience Strategy During Scaling"   NÚMEROS:  Same general rules apply, just faster timelines   VISUAL:  Manutenção de best practices, velocidade acelerada de execução   [85:55] "Would you also launch new creatives, new audiences, or you just keep everything the same and you just increase the budget?"   [86:10] "Try to keep everything the same, but still all the same rules of marketing apply. When you have ad fatigue, you're going to overcome ad fatigue with the normal protocol. You're still going to do the normal things you do."   [86:30] "You're just in most instances having to operate a little bit faster. Timelines are compressing when you do rapid scaling. So just anticipate having to do more in less time. Same general best practices, you're just doing things faster and spending more money over a shorter duration of time."   [86:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Scaling Back Down - KPI Recovery"   NÚMEROS:  Client example: $4,500/day scaled down to $1,500/day, cost per call improved, Black Friday CPM impact   VISUAL:  Demonstração de KPI recovery quando se reduz spend, efeito reversível   [87:00] "Have you found that when you actually scale the budget inside the initial campaign, does going back to the initial budget before the scaling test, does it make your KPI go back to where they were before?"   [87:20] "Yeah, typically. A good example, like we have a client that we had scaled all the way up to about 4,500 a day on a VSL call funnel. There was no availability for the closers throughout these last couple days."   [87:40] "So, I had to scale it all the way down to about 1,500 a day and my cost per call got better as a result of doing that. And then now I'm incrementally pushing it back up to the 4,500 a day."   [88:00] "It'll probably take me till about Tuesday or Wednesday to get to that level. And just as a result of Black Friday weekend, we're seeing CPMs drop as well. So, even though we're scaling it back up to $4,500, we're actually still getting a better cost per call."   [88:25] "I'm sure it'll flux up again because of Santa, but that'll take a couple days into December and it won't be as bad. It's not as concentrated of a high spend window."   [88:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Data Analysis Timeline Question"   NÚMEROS:  No specific timeline - depends on what breaks   VISUAL:  Decision framework baseado em breakage vs success, não em tempo fixo   [88:55] "How long do you actually look at the data to conclude whether or not the test was successful?"   [89:10] "Well if something doesn't break then you just keep scaling. If something does break then you conclude whether it can be fixed or not or whether you need to scale back down and fix it after you scaled down."   [89:30] "Scaling down just means you slow everything down. You remove a substantial amount of data flowing through whatever that step is and therefore you give yourself a higher probability to fix it without a tremendous amount of financial loss."   [89:50] "If I fix something while it's way up here in daily spend the amount of money I'm losing as a result of whatever is broken is tremendously greater than if I scale it down and fix it down here."   [90:10] "You'd want to talk to the business owner and let them make the choice. Do you want me to solve it up here or do you want me to pull things back and solve it down here? Usually a collective decision to be fair."   [90:30] "Ideally nothing breaks and ideally you can just keep scaling it up."   [90:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Ash's Agency Question - Fear-Based Decisions"   NÚMEROS:  4 clients in past month, 10 accounts per team member, current small scale   VISUAL:  Exemplo de decisão baseada em medo vs realidade, operational complexity concerns   [90:55] "Ash: I often over complicate stuff. I go into a lot of detail and that's good with my clients but it's bad - I just took on like four clients in the past month and already I stop my ads because I see that the potential for one of my guys leaves he's managing 10 accounts."   [91:20] "I've got some training. I've got some playbooks. So, there's all this operational complexity even on a small scale where my agency is not doing anywhere near as much as what some of the guys in this room."   [91:40] "I'm trying to figure out because I'd love to scale. I'm trying to figure out how to simplify the operations, how to simplify that side of stuff so that I can with a service-based offering."   [92:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Universal Advice - Not Info Product Specific"   NÚMEROS:  Applicable to all businesses: info products, services, high ticket, any business model   VISUAL:  Clarificação de aplicabilidade universal dos princípios apresentados   [92:05] "First of all, let me be clear. Nothing I talk about is specific to info products is specific to high ticket products, services, any business to be clear can follow this advice. It's applicable to everything. Don't frame it as that."   [92:30] "Also it's important to note choosing to scale down limits the company's resources. You don't have anything that broke. There's a very key thing to note about what you said. You made a decision out of fear, not out of the fact that something broke."   [92:50] "You made a decision out of a potential issue, not out of an actual issue. Choosing to willingly limit your company resources is essentially choosing to limit what you can do if and when a problem does come up that you need to solve."   [93:15] "The more resources that a company has, the easier it is to solve problems and typically the better that they can solve those problems. You turning off your ads in that example is you willingly limiting your resources that you're going to give available to yourself."   [93:40] "I think that's a terrible choice. I think it makes absolute sense to continue scaling, to continue accumulating more resources and continuing to run into real walls, not made-up walls, not hypothetical walls, real walls and real ceilings, real problems that you then need to solve."   [94:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Ash's Real Breaking Points Identified"   NÚMEROS:  14 hour workdays, premium client service quality dropping, ad fatigue occurred, insufficient team training   VISUAL:  Identificação de real problems vs imagined problems, legitimate stress test failure   [94:15] "Ash: The only thing I would say is in this case what broke was I ended up having to work like 14 hours a day and then secondly I wasn't giving enough of bespoke service to the premium clients that I just signed."   [94:35] "I hadn't trained my staff enough to be able to give that premium service and that's why I stopped the ads and even then I stopped the ads because the ads got ad fatigue and I didn't have the time to service those premium clients and also record new ads."   [94:55] "I know if I'd recorded the new ads and also if I had the time to record the new ads and also teach my team to service these premium clients and figure out how to service them. Then I could continue. But I couldn't do all those things at once."   [95:15] "Does that count as breaking? Yes, absolutely. That makes - to be clear, now you pull it back. Now you solve those specific problems that you brought up."   [95:35] "You have to obviously optimize around efficiency. Sounds like you're going to have to bring in some new staff. You have to find a way to get the things done faster and still get the same level of result that you're getting now operationally."   [95:55] "And then to be clear, yeah, you do what you need to do to kick it back on and scale it up again and see what breaks next and hopefully nothing does. That was a good decision in that case then."   [96:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Staff Replacement Question - Purple Hair Analogy"   NÚMEROS:  10 core clients at risk, guy threatened to leave then said he's staying   VISUAL:  Poll results: everyone says replace the staff member, poison frog/snake analogy   [96:25] "Ash: I think in terms of training, I'm literally mortified. I had a guy who told me he was about to leave and then he said, 'Oh, I'm not about to leave.' And so I started hiring frantically and I found substitutes for him in case he does."   [96:45] "But even with all the training that we have, if he suddenly leaves, we're not immediately going to provide the same level of quality of service to say 10 of our core clients."   [97:05] "I just wondered whether that's just me being really inexperienced and not having had that before. And I'm sure whether if it happened once my training would carry through the new guy to be able to replace the old guy. Maybe that's just me being afraid."   [97:25] "I think that's a real risk. Everybody put a one in the chat if Ash should replace that staff member, put a two in the chat if you do not agree to replace the staff member."   [97:45] "So, you see the chat, Ash. It's all get rid of that fucking guy. That's an example of a problem that - it's kind of like when a girl has like purple or blue hair and like a fuckload of tattoos."   [98:05] "It's like one of those poison frogs in the environment. It's like a snake that's all crazy colored and indicating to you that it's full of poison. There's signs that something is indicating to you ahead of time I'm going to be problematic."   [98:30] "This is one of those situations where you are getting the grace thankfully of somebody indicating to you I will eventually be a problem. And as a business owner you just have to attack those things when they pop up."   [98:50] "You don't want to sit on things like that for sure.
